one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning cheaper models (GPT-3.5 Turbo and Code Llama 34b) on ScrawlD-labelled Solidity contracts beats zero-shot GPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo at smart-contract vulnerability detection (weighted F1 0.61 and 0.568 vs 0.218/0.243), but only two of eight vulnerability classes are reliably detected and labels rely on noisy tool-based ground truth.
key_finding: Fine-tuned GPT-3.5 Turbo (GPT-3.5FT) was the best model on every aggregate metric: binary-classification F1 0.776 (vs GPT-4 0.66, GPT-4 Turbo 0.675) and weighted F1 0.61 across all eight vulnerabilities (vs GPT-4 0.218, GPT-4 Turbo 0.243). Detect Llama - Foundation was second with weighted F1 0.568, also clearly beating both GPT-4 variants. Detect Llama - Instruct was weaker (weighted F1 0.297), only marginally above the GPT-4 models. The GPTLens re-implementation (best config gt1c) reached weighted F1 0.320, beating raw GPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo but far below the two best fine-tuned models. Adding a critic step to GPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo gave only marginal gains (0.218->0.226 and 0.243->0.255). A striking observation: the authors note fine-tuning GPT-3.5 winning is surprising because OpenAI describes GPT-3.5 fine-tuning as improving steerability/formatting rather than adding knowledge. Detection quality is highly uneven across classes - only Arithmetic (ARTHM) and Locked Ether (LE) are detected well by any model; DOS, TimeM, Tx-Origin and Unhandled Exception are near-zero F1 for almost all models.
core_contribution: The paper tests the hypothesis that a fine-tuned open-source code LLM can outperform GPT-4 at Solidity smart-contract vulnerability detection. The authors fine-tune two Code Llama 34b variants (Detect Llama - Foundation and Detect Llama - Instruct) on 17,000 prompts derived from the ScrawlD labelled-contract dataset, additionally fine-tune OpenAI's GPT-3.5 Turbo on a 4,000-prompt subset, and evaluate all of these plus a random baseline against zero-shot GPT-4 / GPT-4 Turbo and a re-implemented GPTLens auditor-critic pipeline. They release the two Detect Llama models and their training/evaluation prompt sets. The headline own-result: fine-tuned GPT-3.5 Turbo and Detect Llama - Foundation both beat GPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo, with the fine-tuned GPT-3.5 the overall winner. NOTE: BlockGPT (Gai et al.), GPTLens's original design (Hu et al.), David et al.'s few-shot GPT-4/Claude study, ESCORT and the LSTM detector (Tann et al.) are cited RELATED work, not contributions of this paper; the paper's own contribution is the fine-tuning + comparative evaluation and the released artifacts.
threat_model: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in deployed Ethereum smart contracts for financial gain (the paper cites ~148 blockchain exploits worth ~USD$4.28B and the 2016 DAO attack as motivation). The defender is a developer/auditor who wants to detect the presence of eight specific vulnerability classes (from the SWC registry / ScrawlD) in Solidity source before or after deployment: Locked Ether, Arithmetic (over/underflow), Denial of Service, Reentrancy, Time Manipulation, Timestamp Ordering, tx.origin authorization, and Unhandled Exception. The paper frames its tool as a fast middle-ground detector between fast-but-false-positive static analysis and accurate-but-slow dynamic analysis/fuzzing. It is a detection/classification tool, not an exploitation or defense-hardening mechanism; adversarial robustness of the detector itself is not studied.
limitations: (1) Test set is restricted to legacy Solidity 0.4.x only (because the labelling tools were not updated), limiting real-world generality; authors flag this as future work. (2) Ground-truth labels - both training and test - come from a majority vote of imperfect static/dynamic tools, so the models are learning to match noisy tool labels, not verified ground truth. (3) The random baseline is a strong reference on binary classification (F1 0.618, accuracy 0.5), showing the binary metric is weak; and Detect Llama - Foundation achieves its high recall (0.993) at very low precision (0.517) and near-zero specificity (0.023) - i.e., it flags almost everything as vulnerable. (4) Only two of the eight vulnerability classes (ARTHM, LE) are detected meaningfully; performance on the rest is close to zero across all models. (5) GPT-4 was closed to fine-tuning at the time, so the comparison is fine-tuned-open/3.5 vs zero-shot GPT-4, not a like-for-like fine-tuning comparison. (6) Reported numbers are internally slightly inconsistent (abstract cites DL-Foundation weighted F1 0.56/0.586 while Table 3 gives 0.568). No robustness, latency, or cost evaluation of the deployed detector.

datasets_used: ScrawlD (Yashavant et al.) - 9,252 real Ethereum Solidity contracts, 5,364 with >=1 vulnerability, labelled by 5-tool majority vote over 8 vulnerabilities
17,000 fine-tuning prompts (detection + generation, Alpaca-Instruct style) derived from ScrawlD contracts pulled via Etherscan verified-contract API
4,000-prompt detection-only subset (ChatGPT format) used to fine-tune GPT-3.5 Turbo
Authors' own test set: ~600 top open-source Solidity 0.4.x contracts, re-labelled by majority vote of Osiris, Oyente, Mythril, Slither, SmartCheck
quantitative_results: Binary classification F1 (Table 2): GPT-3.5FT 0.776, GPTLens 0.692, DL-Foundation 0.68, GPT-4 Turbo 0.675, GPT-4 0.66, Random 0.618, DL-Instruct 0.563
DL-Foundation binary precision 0.517 / recall 0.993 / specificity 0.023 (flags almost everything vulnerable)
Weighted F1 over all 8 vulnerabilities (Table 3): GPT-3.5FT 0.61, DL-Foundation 0.568, GPTLens 0.320, DL-Instruct 0.297, GPT-4 Turbo 0.243, GPT-4 0.218, random 0.184
Top-2 vulnerabilities (ARTHM + LE) weighted F1 (Table 4): GPT-3.5FT 0.719, DL-Foundation 0.674, GPTLens 0.441, GPT-4 Turbo 0.429, GPT-4 0.363
Per-vulnerability F1 for GPT-3.5FT: ARTHM 0.639, LE 0.81, RENT 0.185, TimeO 0.219, DOS/TimeM/Tx-Origin/UE ~0
Critic step gave only marginal gains: GPT-4 0.218->0.226, GPT-4 Turbo 0.243->0.255 (weighted F1)
GPT-3.5 fine-tune trained on 16,906,389 tokens over 3 epochs; Code Llama fine-tuned with 7500-token context, 3 epochs, QLoRA + Flash Attention V2
Serving a 34b Detect Llama model with Huggingface TGI requires a single A100 80GB GPU
baselines_compared: GPT-4 Critic and GPT-4 Turbo Critic (auditor->critic step)
GPTLens (re-implemented Hu et al. auditor+critic; best config gt1c)
Random baseline (each contract assigned 0-4 random vulnerabilities)
Detect Llama - Instruct (also a fine-tuned model of the authors)
